senses.definition: A historical group of wingless insects, used in older scientific classifications of insects.
examples: In older textbooks, insects were sometimes divided into Apterygogenea and Pterygogenea.
The term Apterygogenea is now mainly of historical interest in entomology.

senses.definition: A former subclass of primitive wingless insects that never had wings, such as bristletails and silverfish.
examples: Apterygota were once treated as a separate subclass of wingless insects.
Modern classifications no longer recognize Apterygota as a natural group.

senses.definition: Any primitive wingless insect that belongs or used to belong to the group Apterygota.
examples: Apterygotes such as springtails and bristletails lack wings at all life stages.
Fossils show that apterygotes appeared very early in insect evolution.

senses.definition: Relating to the primitive wingless insects called Apterygota.
examples: Apterygote insects do not undergo a dramatic metamorphosis.
The apterygote condition is thought to be ancestral among insects.

senses.definition: Belonging to or having the characteristics of the Apterygota, especially being primitively wingless.
examples: These apterygotous insects retain the same form throughout their life.
Apterygotous species lack the wing pads seen in young winged insects.

senses.definition: An area of a bird's skin between feather tracts where no normal contour feathers grow.
examples: Down feathers may occur in the apteryla between the main feather tracts.
The pattern of pterylae and apteryla helps identify bird species in anatomical studies.

senses.definition: A kiwi, a small, nocturnal, flightless bird from New Zealand with a long bill.
examples: The apteryx is a national symbol of New Zealand and is better known as the kiwi.
Unlike most birds, the apteryx has nostrils near the tip of its long beak.

senses.definition: Plural form of apteryx, meaning more than one kiwi bird.
examples: Several apteryxes were sighted in the forest reserve during the night survey.
The sanctuary breeds apteryxes to help protect the species from extinction.

senses.definition: Most suitable or most appropriate for a particular situation.
examples: Of all the candidates, she was the aptest choice for the role.
The aptest description of the weather today is simply miserable.

senses.definition: Most quick to learn or understand.
examples: He was the aptest student in the class and quickly grasped complex ideas.
The aptest learners benefit greatly from challenging tasks.

senses.definition: Relating to a stage of the Early Cretaceous period in geology.
examples: The fossils were dated to the Aptian stage of the Early Cretaceous.
Aptian sediments provide evidence of major climate changes in Earth's history.

senses.definition: The Aptian stage of the Early Cretaceous period.
examples: These rocks were laid down during the Aptian.
The Aptian is followed by the Albian in the Cretaceous sequence.

senses.definition: A technical or specialized name likely related to the Aptian stage (for example, a formation or locality name in geology).
examples: The fossils were collected from the Aptiana beds described in the regional survey.
Researchers compared Aptiana samples with those from nearby formations.

senses.definition: Present participle of a rare sense of 'apt', meaning to make something suitable or to fit something to a purpose.
examples: She spent the afternoon apting the old room for use as a study.
The tool is useful for apting the design to different user groups.

senses.definition: A natural ability or skill, especially in learning something quickly and easily.
examples: She has a strong aptitude for languages and learns new words quickly.
The company tests applicants for their aptitude in problem solving.

senses.definition: Plural of aptitude: several natural abilities or skills, especially in particular areas.
examples: Different students have different aptitudes and learning styles.
The assessment identifies your main aptitudes and interests.

senses.definition: Relating to aptitude or natural ability.
examples: The school offers aptitudinal tests to guide students' career choices.
Aptitudinal differences can influence how quickly learners progress.

senses.definition: In a way that relates to aptitude or natural ability.
examples: The students in this class are quite varied aptitudinally.
He is aptitudinally suited to analytical work rather than sales.

senses.definition: In a way that is suitable or appropriate for the situation.
examples: The restaurant is aptly named 'Sunset View' because of its location.
She aptly described the meeting as 'lively but chaotic'.

senses.definition: The quality of being suitable or appropriate in a particular situation.
examples: Critics praised the aptness of the film's title.
He questioned the aptness of using that example in a beginner's class.

senses.definition: The quality of having a natural ability to do something.
examples: Her aptness for music was clear from an early age.
The test measures the aptness of candidates for technical work.

senses.definition: Plural of aptness: several instances of being suitable or of having natural ability.
examples: The committee discussed the aptnesses of the different examples chosen.
Different aptnesses in language and numbers make the class heterogeneous.

senses.definition: In grammar, a word that does not change its form to show different grammatical cases.
examples: In some languages, certain loanwords function as an aptote and have the same form in all cases.
The noun behaves as an aptote, so learners do not need to memorize case endings.

senses.definition: Plural of aptote: words that do not change form for grammatical case.
examples: Proper names are often treated as aptotes in that language.
These loanwords are aptotes and share a single form in all case functions.

senses.definition: Describing a word that does not change its form to mark different grammatical cases.
examples: An aptotic noun has the same ending in all case positions.
The language has both fully inflected nouns and aptotic ones.

senses.definition: Third person singular of 'apt' in the sense of 'is likely' or 'tends' (for example, 'he apts to'). Note that this form is extremely rare and non‑standard.
examples: In dialect use, one might say 'he apts to forget his keys', meaning he tends to forget them.
The phrase 'she apts to worry' would sound non-standard to most speakers.

senses.definition: Informal short form for 'apartments'.
examples: The new complex has luxury apts with great views.
Check the listing for available apts in the city center.

senses.definition: A medical condition in which the salivary glands produce less saliva than normal.
examples: Aptyalia can cause difficulty in chewing and swallowing food.
Certain medications may lead to aptyalia in older patients.

senses.definition: A medical condition in which there is a complete lack of saliva.
examples: In aptyalism, patients may suffer from severe dryness of the mouth.
Aptyalism can result from damage to the salivary glands or certain medical treatments.

senses.definition: A hard plate found as a fossil, thought to be part of the jaw or shell cover of some extinct marine animals such as ammonites.
examples: The fossil layer contained several well-preserved aptychi associated with ammonite shells.
Paleontologists still debate the exact function of the aptychus in ammonites.

senses.definition: Relating to Apulia, a region in southern Italy.
examples: They enjoyed Apulian cuisine during their holiday in southern Italy.
Several Apulian towns are famous for their whitewashed houses.

senses.definition: A person from Apulia, a region in southern Italy.
examples: An Apulian showed us the best local restaurants.
Many Apulians work in agriculture and tourism.

senses.definition: Having no lungs.
examples: Some apulmonic amphibians absorb oxygen through their skin.
Apulmonic animals must rely on other structures for gas exchange.

senses.definition: Without a detectable pulse.
examples: The patient arrived apulse and required immediate resuscitation.
If a person remains apulse for several minutes, survival chances fall sharply.

senses.definition: On purpose, intentionally.
examples: He did not spill the drink apurpose; it was an accident.
She spoke sharply, but not apurpose.

senses.definition: A small constellation in the southern sky, named after the bird of paradise.
examples: Apus is a faint constellation visible only from southern latitudes.
Few bright stars lie within Apus, so it can be hard to locate.

senses.definition: Informal written abbreviation of 'approximately'.
examples: The trip will take apx 3 hours by car.
There were apx 50 people at the event.

senses.definition: A chemical compound or substance referred to in specialized scientific literature (exact nature varies by source).
examples: The reaction produced small amounts of apyonin as a by-product.
Researchers measured the effect of apyonin on enzyme activity.
senses.__comment: Note: field '__comment' is not allowed by user's schema; removing it.

senses.definition: An enzyme that breaks down ATP and ADP into AMP and phosphate, often used to stop certain reactions in biology.
examples: Apyrase is used in the lab to remove excess ATP from the reaction mixture.
Salivary apyrases in blood-sucking insects help prevent blood clotting.

senses.definition: Plural of apyrase: several enzymes that break down ATP and ADP into AMP and phosphate.
examples: Different apyrases show different preferences for ATP or ADP.
Salivary apyrases of mosquitoes are targets for anti-clotting research.

senses.definition: Describing a sperm cell or pollen grain that does not contain a nucleus or genetic material.
examples: Some insects produce both fertile and apyrene sperm.
Apyrene grains do not participate directly in fertilization.

senses.definition: Not having a fever.
examples: By the third day of treatment, the patient was apyretic.
An apyretic child may still feel unwell despite having no fever.

senses.definition: A period during an illness when the patient has no fever.
examples: The disease is marked by alternating days of fever and apyrexia.
During apyrexia, the patient feels relatively well.

senses.definition: Relating to or marked by the absence of fever.
examples: The patient entered an apyrexial phase after starting antibiotics.
An apyrexial interval separated two fever spikes.

senses.definition: Plural of apyrexia: several periods in which a patient does not have a fever.
examples: The illness is characterized by short fevers separated by long apyrexias.
Repeated apyrexias may give a false impression of recovery.

senses.definition: The absence of fever.
examples: After three days of apyrexy, the doctors considered discharging her.
Apyrexy is a key sign that the infection is under control.

senses.definition: An early type of photographic print made without the use of heat in the developing process.
examples: The museum holds a rare apyrotype of the town square from the 19th century.
Apyrotypes were among several experimental photographic techniques of the period.

senses.definition: Not affected by fire or heat; resistant to burning.
examples: The apyrous lining protected the structure during the blaze.
Engineers looked for apyrous materials for the furnace.

senses.definition: Written abbreviation for 'aqueous', used especially in chemistry to show that a substance is dissolved in water.
examples: In the equation, NaCl(aq) means salt dissolved in water.
The reaction occurs in an aq solution at room temperature.

senses.definition: Water, especially when used as a technical term or in product names.
examples: The main ingredient listed on the label is aqua, meaning water.
In Latin, 'aqua' simply means water.

senses.definition: A light blue-green color, like clear tropical water.
examples: She painted the walls a soft aqua.
He wore a shirt in bright aqua and white stripes.

senses.definition: Relating to energetic or acrobatic movements and exercises performed in water.
examples: The resort offers aquabatic classes in the pool every morning.
Aquabatic routines can be both fun and demanding for beginners.

senses.definition: Energetic or acrobatic movements and exercises performed in water, often as a sport or fitness activity.
examples: She teaches aquabatics to seniors at the local pool.
Aquabatics improves strength and flexibility with less impact on the joints.

senses.definition: A woman or girl who performs graceful or acrobatic swimming displays.
examples: She worked as an aquabelle in a seaside resort show.
The film features a team of aquabelles performing synchronized routines.

senses.definition: An old, humorous word for a person who drinks only water and does not drink alcohol.
examples: He joked that he had become an aquabib after giving up wine.
In the 19th century, temperance writers sometimes mocked aquabibs.

senses.definition: A board used for riding on water, wider than a water ski but narrower than a surfboard, usually with both feet on the same board.
examples: He learned to balance on an aquaboard at the lake.
The new aquaboard is designed for beginners and children.

senses.definition: Plural of aquaboard: several boards used for riding on water.
examples: The shop rents out aquaboards and life jackets by the hour.
Different aquaboards are available for tricks or for beginners.
